{
  "markdown": "Slurm Job Manager | Research Computing\n\nPress 'S' to search\n\n# Overview\n\nUVA HPC is a multi-user, managed environment. It is divided into login nodes (also called frontends), which are directly accessible by users, and compute nodes, which must be accessed through the resource manager. Users prepare their computational workloads, called jobs, on the login nodes and submit them to the job controller, a component of the resource manager that runs on login nodes and is responsible for scheduling jobs and monitoring the status of the compute nodes.\n\nWe use Slurm, an open-source tool that manages jobs for Linux clusters. Jobs are submitted to the Slurm controller, which queues them until the system is ready to run them. The controller selects which jobs to run, when to run them, and how to place them on the compute node or nodes, according to a predetermined site policy meant to balance competing user needs and to maximize efficient use of cluster resources. Slurm divides a cluster into logical units called partitions (generally known as queues in other systems). Different partitions may contain different nodes, or they may overlap; they may also impose different resource limitations. The UVA HPC environment provides several partitions and there is no default; each job must request a partition. To determine which queues are available, log in to the HPC System and type\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nqlist\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nat a Linux command-line prompt. For the memory and core limits on each queue, use the command\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nqlimits\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n# Local Queue Configuration\n\nSeveral queues (partitions) are available for different types of jobs. One queue is restricted to single-node (serial or threaded) jobs; another for multinode parallel programs, and others are for access to specialty hardware such as large-memory nodes or nodes offering GPUs. For the current queue configuration and policies on UVA HPC please see its homepage.\n\n# Slurm Architecture\n\nSlurm has a controller process (called a daemon) on a head node and a worker daemon on each of the compute nodes. The controller is responsible for queueing jobs, monitoring the state of each node, and allocating resources. The worker daemon gathers information about its node and returns that information to the controller. When assigned a user job by the controller, the worker daemon initiates and manages the job. Slurm provides the interface between the user and the cluster. Slurm performs three primary tasks:\n\n1. Manage the queue(s) of jobs and settles contentions for resources;\n2. Allocate a subset of nodes or cores for a set amount of time to a submitted job;\n3. Provide a framework for starting and monitoring jobs on the subset of nodes/cores.\n\nTo submit a job to the cluster, you must request the appropriate resources and specify what you want to run with a Slurm Job Command File. In most cases, this batch job file is simply a bash or other shell script containing directives that specify the resource requirements (e.g. the number of cores, the maximum runtime, partition specification, etc.) that your job is requesting along with the set of commands required to execute your workflow on a subset of cluster compute nodes. Batch job scripts are submitted to the Slurm Controller to be run on the cluster. When the script is submitted to the resource manager, the controller reads the directives, ignoring the rest of the script, and uses them to determine the overall resource request. It then assigns a priority to the job and places it into the queue. Once the job is assigned to a worker, the job script is run as an ordinary shell script on the “master” node, in which case the directives are treated as comments. For this reason it is important to follow the format for directives exactly.\n\nThe remainder of this tutorial will focus on the Slurm command line interface. More detailed information about using Slurm can be found in the [official Slurm documentation](https://slurm.schedmd.com/documentation.html).\n\n# Job Scripts\n\nJobs are submitted through a job script, which is a shell script (usually written in&#96;bash&#96;). Since it is a shell script it must begin with a “shebang”\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n#!/bin/bash\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThis is followed by a preamble describing the resource requests the job is making. Each request begins with&#96;#SBATCH&#96; followed by an option.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n#SBATCH --time=12:00:00\n#SBATCH -A myaccount\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nAfter all resource requests have been established through&#96;#SBATCH&#96;, the script must describe exactly how to run the job.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nmodule purge\nmodule load gcc\n./mycode\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nIn our installation of Slurm, the default starting directory is the directory from which the batch job was submitted, so it may not be necessary to change directories.\n\n---\n\n# Configurable Options in Slurm\n\nSlurm refers to processes as “tasks.” A task may be envisioned as an independent, running process. Slurm also refers to cores as “cpus” even though modern cpus contain several to many cores. If your program uses only one core it is a single, sequential task. If it can use multiple cores on the same node, it is generally regarded as a single task, with multiple cores assigned to that task. If it is a distributed code that can run on multiple nodes, each process would be a task.\n\n## Options\n\nNote that most Slurm options have two forms, a short (single-letter) form that is preceded by a single hyphen and followed by a space, and a longer form preceded by a double hyphen and followed by an equals sign. In a job script these options are preceded by a pseudocomment&#96;#SBATCH&#96;. They may also be used as command-line options on their own.\n\n| Option | Usage |\n| --- | --- |\n| Number of nodes | &#96;-N &#96; or&#96;--nodes= &#96; |\n| Number of tasks | &#96;-n &#96; or&#96;--ntasks= &#96; |\n| Number of processes (tasks) per node | &#96;--ntasks-per-node= &#96; |\n| Total memory per node in megabytes | &#96;--mem= &#96; |\n| Memory per core in megabytes | &#96;--mem-per-cpu= &#96; |\n| Wallclock time | &#96;-t d-hh:mm:ss&#96; or&#96;--time=d-hh:mm:ss&#96; |\n| Partition (queue) requested | &#96;-p &#96; or&#96;--partition &#96; |\n| Account to be charged | &#96;-A &#96; or&#96;--account= &#96; |\n\nThe --mem and --mem-per-cpu options are mutually exclusive. Job scripts should specify one or the other but not both.\n\n---\n\n## Environment variables\n\nThese are the most basic; there are many more. By default Slurm changes to the directory from which the job was submitted, so the&#96;SLURM_SUBMIT_DIR&#96; environment variable is usually not needed.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nSLURM_JOB_ID\nSLURM_SUBMIT_DIR\nSLURM_JOB_PARTITION\nSLURM_JOB_NODELIST\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nSlurm passes all environment variables from the shell in which the&#96;sbatch&#96; or&#96;salloc&#96;(or&#96;ijob&#96;) command was run. To override this behavior in an&#96;sbatch&#96; script, add\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n#SBATCH --export=NONE\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nTo export specific variables use\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n#SBATCH --export=var1,var2,var3\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nOptionally export and set with\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n#SBATCH --export=variable1=value1,variable2=value2\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Standard Output and Standard Error\n\nBy default, Slurm combines standard output and standard error into a single file, which will be named slurm-.out. You may rename standard output with\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n#SBATCH -o \u003coutfile\u003e or --output=\u003coutfile\u003e\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nTo separate standard error from standard output, you must rename both.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n#SBATCH -e \u003cerrfile\u003e or --error=\u003cerrfile\u003e\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n# Slurm Script Generator\n\nIf you would like assistance in generating Slurm scripts, please check out our [Slurm Script Generator](https://www.rc.virginia.edu/userinfo/hpc/slurm-script-generator). Simply input the parameters of your job to get a fully-working Slurm script.\n\n# Submitting a Job\n\nJob scripts are submitted with the sbatch command, e.g.:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ sbatch hello.slurm\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThe job identification number is returned when you submit the job, e.g.:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ sbatch hello.slurm\nSubmitted batch job 18341\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n# Submitting an Interactive Job\n\nIf you wish to run a job directly from the shell, you can run an interactive job. If you are using any kind of graphical user interface (GUI) you should use one of the [Open OnDemand](https://www.rc.virginia.edu/userinfo/hpc/ood) interactive apps. This offers direct access to Jupyterlab, VSCode Server, RStudio Server, the MATLAB desktop, and others. For graphical applications not available through one of the dedicated apps, such as the Totalview debugger or some bioinformatics packages, use the Open OnDemand [Desktop](https://www.rc.virginia.edu/userinfo/hpc/ood/desktop) app. From the Desktop you can open a terminal window, load modules, and start any application you wish. Please note that a few GUI applications require a GPU so you must request that partition in the online form.\n\nIf you wish to run an interactive job from the command line, you can use our local command&#96;ijob&#96; to obtain a login shell on a compute node.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ ijob \u003coptions\u003e\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nijob is a wrapper around the Slurm commands salloc and srun, set up to start a bash shell on the remote node. The options are the same as the options to salloc, so most commands that can be used with #SBATCH can be used with ijob. The request will be placed into the queue specified:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ ijob -c 1 -A mygroup -p standard --time=1-00:00:00\nsalloc: Pending job allocation 25394\nsalloc: job 25394 queued and waiting for resources\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThere may be some delay for the resource to become available.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nsalloc: job 25394 has been allocated resources\nsalloc: Granted job allocation 25394\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nFor all interactive jobs, the allocated node(s) will remain reserved as long as the terminal session is open, up to the walltime limit, so it is extremely important that users exit their interactive sessions as soon as their work is done sothat the user is not charged for unused time. If you are using an interactive app, be sure to&#96;delete session&#96; if you exit the session before time runs out. In the case of a command-line ijob, the job will be terminated if you exit your shell for any reason, including shutting down the local computer from which your login originates.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ exit \nsalloc: Relinquishing job allocation 25394\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nA full list of options is available from SchedMD’s salloc documentation, or you may run\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nijob --help\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nat the command line.\n\n# Displaying Job Status\n\nThe squeue command is used to obtain status information about all jobs submitted to all queues. Without any specified options, the squeue command provides a display which is similar to the following:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nJOBID     PARTITION     NAME       USER    ST  TIME      NODES    NODELIST(REASON)\n\n----------------------------------------------------------------------------------\n\n12345     parallel      myHello    mst3k   R   5:31:21   4        udc-ba33-4a, udc-ba33-4b, uds-ba35-22d, udc-ba39-16a\n\n12346     standard      bash       mst3k   R   2:44      1        udc-ba30-5\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThe fields of the display are clearly labeled, and most are self-explanatory. The TIME field indicates the elapsed walltime (hrs:min:secs) that the job has been running. Note that JOBID 12346 has the name bash, which indicates it is an interactive job. In that case, the TIME field provides the amount of walltime during which the interactive session has to be open (and resources have been allocated). The ST field lists a code which indicates the state of the job. Commonly listed states include:\n\n- PD PENDING: Job is waiting for resources;\n- R RUNNING: Job has the allocated resources and is running;\n- S SUSPENDED: Job has the allocated resources, but execution has been suspended.\n\nA complete list of job state codes is available here.\n\nTo check on your job’s status\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ sstat \u003cjobid\u003e\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nFor detailed information\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ scontrol show job \u003cjobid\u003e\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nTo request an estimate of when your pending job will run\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ squeue --start -j \u003cjobid\u003e\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n# Canceling a Job\n\nSlurm provides the scancel command for deleting jobs from the system using the job identification number:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ scancel 18341\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nIf you did not note the job identification number (JOBID) when it was submitted, you can use squeue to retrieve it.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ squeue -u mst3k\n\nJOBID     PARTITION      NAME         USER     ST    TIME   NODES    NODELIST(REASON)\n-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------\n18341     standard          myHello      mst3k    R     0:01   1        udc-ba30-5\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nTo cancel all of your jobs\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ scancel -u mst3k\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nTo cancel all of your pending jobs\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ scancel -t PENDING -u mst3k\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nTo cancel all jobs with a specified name\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ scancel --name myjob\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nTo restart (cancel and rerun)\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ scontrol requeue \u003cjobid\u003e\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nFor further information about the squeue command, type&#96;man squeue&#96; on the cluster front-end machine or see the Slurm Documentation.\n\n# Job Arrays\n\nA large number of jobs can be submitted through one request if all the files used follow a strict pattern. For example, if input files are named input_1.dat, … , input_1000.dat, we could write a job script requesting the appropriate resources for a single one of these jobs with\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n#!/bin/bash\n#SBATCH --ntasks=1\n#SBATCH --time=1:00:00\n#SBATCH --output=result_%a.out\n#SBATCH --partition=standard\n\n/myprogram \u003c input_${SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID}.dat\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nIn the output file name, %a is the placeholder for the array ID. We submit with\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ sbatch --array=1-1000 myjob.sh\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThe system automatically submits 1000 jobs, which will all appear under a single job ID with separate array IDs. The SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID environment variable can be used in your command lines to label individual subjobs.\n\nThe placeholder %A stands for the overall job ID number in the #SBATCH preamble lines, while %a represents the individual task number. These variables can be used with the –output option. In the body of the script you can use the regular environment variable SLURM_TASK_ID if you wish to differentiate different job IDs and SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID for the jobs within the array.\n\nTo submit a range of task IDs with an interval\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ sbatch --array=1-1000:2\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nTo submit a list of task IDs\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ sbatch --array=1,3,9,11,22\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Using Files with Job Arrays\n\nFor more complex commands, you can prepare a file containing the text you wish to use. Your job script can read the file line by line. In the following example, you must number your subtasks starting from 1 sequentially. You must prepare the&#96;options_file.txt&#96; in advance and each line must be the options you wish to pass to your program.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n#!/bin/bash\n#\n#SBATCH --ntasks=1\n#SBATCH --partition=standard\n#SBATCH --time=3:00:00\n#SBATCH --array=1-1000\n\nOPTS=$(sed -n \"${SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID}\"p options.txt)\n\n./myprogram $OPTS\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThe double quotes and curly braces are required.\n\n## Canceling Individual Tasks in an Array\n\nOne task\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ scancel \u003cjobid\u003e_\u003ctaskid\u003e\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nA range of tasks\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ scancel \u003cjobid\u003e_[\u003ctaskid1\u003e-\u003ctaskid2\u003e]\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nA list of tasks\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ scancel \u003cjobid\u003e_[\u003ctaskid1\u003e,\u003ctaskid2\u003e,\u003ctaskid3\u003e]\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n# Specifying Job Dependencies\n\nWith the sbatch command, you can invoke options that prevent a job from starting until a previous job has finished. This constraint is especially useful when a job requires an output file from another job in order to perform its tasks. The –dependency option allows for the specification of additional job attributes. For example, suppose that we have two jobs where job_2 must run after job_1 has completed. Using the corresponding Slurm command files, we can submit the jobs as follows:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nsbatch job_1.slurm \nSubmitted batch job 18375 \nsbatch --dependency=afterok:18375 job_2.slurm\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nNotice that the –dependency has its own condition, in this case afterok. We want job_2 to start only after the job with id 18375 has completed successfully. The afterok condition specifies that dependency. Other commonly-used conditions include the following:\n\n- after: The dependent job is started after the specified job_id starts running;\n- afterany: The dependent job is started after the specified job_id terminates either successfully or with a failure;\n- afternotok: The dependent job is started only if the specified job_id terminates with a failure.\n\nMore options for arguments of the dependency condition are detailed in the manual pages for sbatch found here or by typing&#96;man sbatch&#96; at the Linux command prompt.\n\nWe also are able to see that a job dependency exists when we view the job status listing, although the explicit dependency is not stated, e.g.:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n% squeue\nJOBID    PARTITION     NAME      USER     ST     TIME    NODES   NODELIST(REASON)\n---------------------------------------------------------------------------------\n18375    standard      job_2.sl  mst3k    PD     0:00    1       (Dependency)\n18374    standard      job_1.sl  ms53k    R      0:09    1       udc-ba30-5\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n# Job Accounting Data\n\nWhen submitting a job to the cluster for the first time, the walltime requirement should be overestimated to ensure that Slurm does not terminate the job prematurely. After the job completes, you can use sacct to get the total time that the job took. Without any specified options, the sacct command provides a display which is similar to the following:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nJobID    JobName        Partition    Account    AllocCPUS   State       ExitCode\n------------------------------------------------------------------------\n18347    hello2.sl+     standard     default    1           COMPLETED    0:0\n18347    .batch         standard     default    1           COMPLETED    0:0\n18348    hello2.sl+     standard     default    1           COMPLETED    0:0\n18348    .batch         standard     default    1           COMPLETED    0:0\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nTo include the total time, you will need to customize the output by using the format options. For example, the command\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n% sacct --format=jobID --format=jobname --format=Elapsed --format=state\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nyields the following display:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nJobID         JobName     Elapsed    State\n------------------------------------------\n18347         hello2.sl+  00:54:59   COMPLETED\n18347.batch   batch       00:54:59   COMPLETED\n18347.0       orted       00:54:59   COMPLETED\n18348         hello2.sl+  00:54:74   COMPLETED\n18348.batch   batch       00:54:74   COMPLETED\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThe Elapsed time is given in hours, minutes, and seconds, with the default format of hh:mm:ss. The Elapsed time can be used as an estimate for the amount of time that you request in future runs; however, there can be differences in timing for a job that is run several times. In the above example, the job called python took 21 minutes, 27 seconds to run the first time (JobID 18352.0) and 16 minutes, 8 seconds the last time (JobID 18353.2). Because the same job can take varying amounts of time to run, it would be prudent to increase Elapsed time by 10% to 25% for future walltime requests. Requesting a little extra time will help to ensure that the time does not expire before a job completes\n\n# Sample Slurm Command Scripts\n\nIn this section are a selection of sample Slurm command files for different types of jobs. For more details on running specific software packages, please see the software pages.\n\n## Basic Serial Program\n\nThis example is for running your own serial (single-core) program. It assumes your program is in the same folder from which your job script was submitted.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n#!/bin/bash\n#SBATCH -N 1\n#SBATCH -n 1\n#SBATCH -t 10:00:00\n#SBATCH -p standard\n#SBATCH -A mygroup\n\n./mycode.exe\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### MATLAB\n\nThis example is for a serial (one core) Matlab job.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n#!/bin/bash\n#SBATCH -N 1\n#SBATCH -n 1\n#SBATCH -t 01:00:00\n#SBATCH -o output_filename\n#SBATCH -p standard\n#SBATCH -A mygroup\n\nmodule load matlab\n\nmatlab -nojvm -nodisplay -nosplash -singleCompThread -r \"Mymain(myvar1s);exit\"\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Python\n\nThis script runs a Python program.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n#!/bin/bash\n#SBATCH -n 1\n#SBATCH -t 01:00:00\n#SBATCH -o myRprog.out\n#SBATCH -p standard\n#SBATCH -A mygroup\n\nmodule load miniforge\n\npython myscript.py\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### R\n\nThis is a Slurm job command file to run a serial R batch job.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n#!/bin/bash\n#SBATCH -n 1\n#SBATCH -t 01:00:00\n#SBATCH -o myRprog.out\n#SBATCH -p standard\n#SBATCH -A mygroup\n\nmodule load R\n\nRscript myRprog.R\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Job Scripts for Parallel Programs\n\n### Distributed Memory Jobs\n\nIf the executable is a parallel program using the Message Passing Interface (MPI), then it will require multiple processors of the cluster to run. This information is specified in the Slurm nodes resource requirement. The script mpiexec is used to invoke the parallel executable. This example is a Slurm job command file to run a parallel (MPI) job using the OpenMPI implementation:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n#!/bin/bash\n#SBATCH --nodes=2\n#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node=40\n#SBATCH --time=12:00:00\n#SBATCH --output=output_filename\n#SBATCH --partition=parallel\n#SBATCH -A mygroup\n\nmodule load gcc\nmodule load openmpi\n\nmpirun ./parallel_executable\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nIn this example, the Slurm job file is requesting two nodes with sixteen tasks per node (for a total of thirty-two processors). Both OpenMPI and IntelMPI are able to obtain the number of processes and the host list from Slurm, so these are not specified. In general, MPI jobs should use all of a node so we’d recommend ntasks-per-node=40 on the parallel partition, but some codes cannot be distributed in that manner so we are showing a more general example here.\n\nSlurm can also place the job freely if the directives specify only the number of tasks. In this case do not specify a node count. This is not generally recommended, however, as it can have a significant negative impact on performance.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n#!/bin/bash\n#SBATCH -N 2\n#SBATCH --ntasks=8\n#SBATCH --time=12:00:00\n#SBATCH --output=output_filename\n#SBATCH --partition=parallel \n#SBATCH -A mygroup\n\nmodule load gcc\nmodule load openmpi\n\nmpirun ./parallel_executable\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### MPI over an odd number of tasks\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n#!/bin/bash\n#SBATCH --ntasks=97\n#SBATCH --nodes=5\n#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node=20\n#SBATCH --time=12:00:00\n#SBATCH --output=output_filename\n#SBATCH --partition=parallel \n#SBATCH -A mygroup\n\nmodule load gcc\nmodule load openmpi\nmpirun ./parallel_executable\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Threaded Jobs (OpenMP or pthreads)\n\nSlurm considers a task to correspond to a process. Specifying a number of cpus (cores) per node ensures that they are on the same node. Slurm does not set standard environment variables such as OMP_NUM_THREADS or NTHREADS, so the script must transfer that information explicitly. This example is for OpenMP:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n#!/bin/bash\n#SBATCH --ntasks=1\n#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=20\n#SBATCH --time=12:00:00\n#SBATCH --output=output_filename\n#SBATCH --partition=standard\n#SBATCH -A mygroup\n\nmodule load gcc\nexport OMP_NUM_THREADS=$SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK\n./threaded_executable\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Hybrid\n\nThe following example runs a total of 32 MPI processes, 8 on each node, with each task using 5 cores for threading. The total number of cores utilized is thus 160.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n#!/bin/bash\n#SBATCH --nodes=4\n#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node=4\n#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=5\n#SBATCH --time=12:00:00\n#SBATCH --output=output_filename\n#SBATCH --partition=parallel\n#SBATCH -A mygroup\n\nmodule load gcc openmpi\nexport OMP_NUM_THREADS=$SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK\nmpirun ./hybrid_executable\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## GPU Computations\n\nThe&#96;gpu&#96; queue provides access to compute nodes equipped with RTX2080Ti, RTX3090, A6000, V100, and A100 NVIDIA GPU devices.\n\nIn order to use GPU devices, the jobs must to be submitted to the gpu partition and must include the --gres=gpu option.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n#!/bin/bash\n#SBATCH -A mygroup\n#SBATCH --partition=gpu\n#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1\n#SBATCH --ntasks=1\n#SBATCH --time=12:00:00\n\nmodule load apptainer tensorflow/2.13.0\napptainer run --nv $CONTAINERDIR/tensorflow-2.13.0.sif myAI.py\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThe second argument to&#96;gres&#96; can be&#96;rtx2080&#96;,&#96;rtx3090&#96;,&#96;v100&#96;, or&#96;a100&#96; for the different GPU architectures. The third argument to&#96;gres&#96; specifies the number of devices to be requested. If unspecified, the job will run on the first available GPU node with a single GPU device regardless of architecture.\n\nTwo models of NVIDIA A100 GPUs are available; 2 nodes with 40GB of GPU memory per GPU, and 18 nodes with 80GB of memory per GPU. To make a specific request for an 80GB A100 node, please add a constraint to the Slurm script:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n#SBATCH --constraint=a100_80gb\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThis is in addition to requesting an&#96;a100&#96; in the&#96;gres&#96; option.\n\n### MIG GPU Partition\n\nWe’re excited to announce the launch of a new MIG-enabled GPU partition (&#96;gpu-mig&#96;), available after our August 12th, 2025 scheduled maintenance. MIG (Multi-Instance GPU) allows a single NVIDIA A100 80GB GPU device to be subdivided into 7 smaller, isolated “slices” each with approximately 10GB GPU memory so that multiple jobs can run concurrently on one physical card, each with guaranteed memory and compute.\n\nWhy this change?\n\n- Shorter wait times: Smaller jobs no longer block an entire 80 GB GPU.\n- Right-sized resources: Request only what you need—reduce waste, improve fairness.\n- Isolation: MIG slices are hardware-isolated; your job won’t be affected by a noisy neighbor.\n\nHow to use:\n\nUse the following SLURM directives in your Slurm job script\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n#SBATCH --partition=gpu-mig\n#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1 or #SBATCH --gres=gpu:1g.10gb:1 (only single MIG slices allowed per job)\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nOr request the&#96;gpu-mig&#96; partition through Open OnDemand (OOD) platform.\n\nNote: Currently, only one A100 80GB node—configured with 56 total 10GB MIG instances—is available for users to try out and provide feedback. Additional MIG resources may be added in the future if this initial rollout proves successful and demonstrates clear benefits. For now, jobs run in this partition will not be charged any Service Units (SUs).\n\n# CPU and Memory Usage\n\nSometimes it is important to determine if you used all cores effectively and if enough memory was allocated to the job. There are separate Slurm commands for running jobs and completed jobs.\n\n## Running job\n\nUse&#96;sstat&#96; to get CPU and memory usage for a running job.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ sstat -j \u003cjobid\u003e --format=AveCPU,MaxRSS\n    AveCPU     MaxRSS\n---------- ----------\n76-07:19:+  95534696K\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThe CPU efficiency can be calculated by dividing the total core time in&#96;AveCPU&#96; by the number of requested cores and run time. The above example was obtained for a job that had been running for about 4 days on 20 cores. Therefore, the CPU efficiency is:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n(76 + 7/24)\n----------- = 95%\n  4 * 20\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nwhich indicates good usage of all 20 cores.\n\nThe maximum memory used is given by&#96;MaxRSS&#96;(about 91 GB).\n\nFor more options, please read the manual&#96;man sstat&#96;.\n\n## Completed job\n\nThe&#96;seff&#96; command reports the CPU and memory usage of a completed job.\n\nPlease use this for completed jobs only - efficiency statistics may be misleading for running jobs.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ seff \u003cjobid\u003e\nJob ID: \u003cjobid\u003e\nCluster: shen\nUser/Group: mst3k/users\nState: COMPLETED (exit code 0)\nNodes: 1\nCores per node: 10\nCPU Utilized: 4-03:27:36\nCPU Efficiency: 84.11% of 4-22:15:20 core-walltime\nJob Wall-clock time: 11:49:32\nMemory Utilized: 3.65 GB\nMemory Efficiency: 4.16% of 87.89 GB\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nIf you need a more detailed analysis of CPU/memory usage, please contact us for help.\n\n# Usage Report\n\n## PI\n\nThe PI of an allocation account can see SU usage of all group members via the following command:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nlist-group-usage -A \u003cyour_allocation\u003e [-S YYYY-MM-DD] [-E YYYY-MM-DD]\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThe&#96;-S&#96; and&#96;-E&#96; flags for the start date and end date are optional. The default values are, respectively, the beginning of the current month and now. You will see a report as follows:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n################################################################################\n#\n# Includes fund 000 (mst3klab)\n# Generated on Mon Aug 31 00:00:00 2020.\n# Reporting fund activity from 2020-08-01 to Now.\n#\n################################################################################\nBeginning Balance:           100000.000\nEnding Balance:               99000.000\n############################### Debit Summary ##################################\nUser   Jobs   SUs\n------ ------ ---------\nmst1k  500      500.000\nmst2k  200      300.000\nmst3k  100      200.000\n############################### End of Report ##################################\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Non-PI\n\nRegular users can run the previous command, but it will only show your own usage. You may use the&#96;sreport&#96; command for the total CPU time of your group members:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ sreport cluster UserUtilizationByAccount Start=2020-08-01 Accounts=\u003cyour_allocation\u003e -t Hours\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nNote that this may be different from the actual allocation usage, since&#96;sreport&#96; is unaware of our SU charge policy, but can serve as an estimate.\n\nTo find out how many Service Units (SUs) a specific job has consumed, users can run the following command. Here the value under the&#96;Amount&#96; column shows the amount of SUs consumed. The time-frame can be controlled using the&#96;-s&#96;(starting time) and&#96;-e&#96;(end time) flags.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$  mam-list-transactions -a \u003callocation-name\u003e -s 2024-11-01 -e 2024-12-03  # -s:starting date   -e: end date\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n---",
  "url": "https://www.rc.virginia.edu/userinfo/hpc/slurm/"
}

_**Agent (claude-4.6-sonnet-medium-thinking)**_


Two separate things are going on: **where** to run Conda on Rivanna, and **why** your current YAML fails on Linux.

---

### 1. Slurm on UVA — use an interactive job (not the login node)

The [UVA Slurm page](https://www.rc.virginia.edu/userinfo/hpc/slurm/) says login nodes are for prep; real work goes through **Slurm**. For a **shell on a compute node**, they document **`ijob`** (wrapper around `salloc` / `srun`):

```text
$ ijob <options>
```

Example from their docs:

```bash
ijob -c 1 -A mygroup -p standard --time=1-00:00:00
```

You must substitute **your** allocation **`-A`**, choose a **partition** **`-p`** (see **`qlist`** / **`qlimits`** on the cluster), and add **memory** if Conda keeps getting killed, e.g. **`--mem=16384`** or higher.

After the job starts, run:

```bash
module load miniforge
cd ~/Ivy_Net
# then conda or mamba
```

Exit the shell when done so you aren’t charged for idle time.

They also point to **Open OnDemand** for GUI/interactive apps if you prefer that over a plain terminal `ijob`.

---

### 2. Why Mamba said `libintl` / `libintl-devel` “does not exist”

Your **`environment-tenure_net.yml`** was exported from a **Mac** Anaconda layout. It pins a huge list of packages, including **macOS-specific** pieces (e.g. **`libcxx`**, **`appnope`**, and gettext-related names that don’t line up the same on **linux-64**). So the solver is correct: those exact pins **aren’t** valid on Rivanna.

Also remove the line:

```yaml
prefix: /opt/anaconda3/envs/tenure_net
```

when using the file on another machine — that **`prefix`** points at your Mac and should not be in a shared env file.

---

### What to do instead (practical)

**Option A — Minimal Linux-friendly YAML (recommended)**  
Create a **new** file (e.g. `environment-tenure_net_linux.yml`) with **only** `name`, `channels`, and **unpinned** (or lightly pinned) dependencies you actually need: `python=3.11`, `pip`, core scientific stack, `jupyter`, `ipykernel`, `lxml`, `requests`, `tqdm`, `beautifulsoup4`, `python-dotenv`, etc., plus a short **`pip:`** section if needed. Let **conda-forge** resolve **Linux** builds.

**Option B — Bootstrap on Rivanna**  
On an **`ijob`** session:

```bash
conda create -n tenure_net python=3.11 -y
conda activate tenure_net
conda install -c conda-forge numpy pandas scipy matplotlib jupyter ipykernel lxml requests tqdm beautifulsoup4 python-dotenv scikit-learn -y
# add more as needed
```

Later you can **`conda env export --no-builds > environment-tenure_net_linux.yml`** **from Rivanna** so the file matches Linux.

---

**Summary:** Use **`ijob`** (with **`-A`**, **`-p`**, **`--time`**, and enough **`--mem`**) per [UVA’s Slurm guide](https://www.rc.virginia.edu/userinfo/hpc/slurm/). Treat the current **`environment-tenure_net.yml`** as **Mac-specific**; either replace it with a **minimal Linux-oriented** YAML or build the env **on Rivanna** and export a new YAML there. I can draft a **`environment-tenure_net_linux.yml`** tailored to your notebook imports if you want that checked into `Ivy_Net`.

---

_**Agent (claude-4.6-sonnet-medium-thinking)**_

Here’s a concise **way ahead** and what was added to the repo.

### Why Mac and Linux diverge
A full **`conda env export`** (your current `environment-tenure_net.yml`) records **exact builds** and **OS-only** packages (`libcxx`, `appnope`, gettext splits, etc.) and a **`prefix:`** path. That file is meant to **reproduce one machine**, not to move to Rivanna.

### Best practice: one hand-maintained spec
1. **Treat a minimal YAML as the source of truth** — only `name`, `channels`, **`python` minor**, and **logical** dependencies (no full solver dump, **no `prefix:`**).
2. **Let conda/mamba resolve** on each OS (`conda-forge` is the usual choice for consistency).
3. **Pin in two layers:**
   - **Loose / unpinned** on conda for numpy, pandas, scipy, matplotlib, jupyter, lxml, etc.
   - **`pip:`** for packages you want **the same version string** on both sides (your previous pip list), understanding that wheels are still **per-platform** but version pins match.

### Tighter reproducibility (optional)
If you need **lockfile-level** parity: use **[conda-lock](https://github.com/conda/conda-lock)** on this base file to generate **separate lockfiles** for `osx-arm64` and `linux-64`, then install from the lock on each machine. That is the standard way to stay “as identical as possible” without pretending one OS export works on the other.

### HPC habits
- Run **heavy** `conda create` / `mamba create` / big `pip` installs inside a **Slurm allocation** (you already hit login-node **Killed**).
- On Rivanna, **drop IDE-only pip entries** (e.g. `cursor-notebook-mcp`, maybe `fastmcp`) if you do not need them there — same repo, slightly slimmer `pip` list on HPC is normal.

### What I added
A new **`environment-tenure_net.base.yml`** at the workspace root: cross-platform comments, **`conda-forge`**, **Python 3.11**, conda deps for the stack your code uses, and your **previous pip pins** grouped under `pip:`. Use:

```bash
mamba env create -f environment-tenure_net.base.yml
# later:
mamba env update -f environment-tenure_net.base.yml --prune
```

Rename or archive the old **`environment-tenure_net.yml`** locally if you want to avoid accidentally using the Mac-only export for Linux.

If you want, we can next **trim the pip list** to only what the notebook + `tenure_pipeline` imports (smaller surface, fewer pin conflicts) or add a **conda-lock** snippet to your checklist doc when you’re ready to edit that file.

---

_**User**_

ok, instead of git push main origin, I just typed git push by mistake

---

_**Agent (claude-4.6-sonnet-medium-thinking)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The user is asking about a git mistake. They mistakenly typed `git push` instead of `git push origin main`. Running `git push` alone uses the configured upstream branch, which may or may not be what they intended. If they previously ran `git push -u origin main`, then `git push` would correctly target origin/main. Otherwise, it might fail or push to a different default branch. This isn't necessarily a mistake if the upstream was properly configured. `git push` without arguments is correct if the current branch is set to track `origin/main` or the intended remote branch. If it succeeded, it pushed to the default remote and branch for the current branch, matching the result of `git push origin main` if on `main` with proper tracking. If it failed, an error would have occurred.</details></think>

_**Agent (claude-4.6-sonnet-medium-thinking)**_

`git push` with no extra arguments is normal. Git pushes your **current branch** to its **upstream** (the branch it was set to track), usually `origin/main` or `origin/<branch>`.

So if you had already run something like `git push -u origin main` once, or your branch was created tracking `origin/main`, **`git push` does the same job** as `git push origin main`.

**If it completed without errors**, you’re almost certainly fine: the code went to `origin` on the branch that’s configured as upstream.

**Quick checks** (when you’re in the repo):

```bash
git status -sb
git branch -vv
```

The second line shows something like `main abc1234 [origin/main]` — that `[origin/main]` means plain `git push` updates that remote branch.

**If `git push` failed**, paste the message and we can interpret it. The usual mistake is pushing before setting upstream; Git then tells you to run `git push -u origin main` once.

_**Agent (claude-4.6-sonnet-medium-thinking)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The user's `git pull` command failed on Rivanna (Linux) due to uncommitted local changes in the `environment-tenure_net` file.</details></think>

_**Agent (claude-4.6-sonnet-medium-thinking)**_

`git pull` stopped because **Rivanna has uncommitted edits** to `environment-tenure_net.yml`, and Git refuses to overwrite them with what’s on `origin`.

**If you want the repo version you just pushed from your Mac** (the usual case):

```bash
cd ~/Ivy_Net
git diff environment-tenure_net.yml    # optional: see what’s different locally
git restore environment-tenure_net.yml   # discard local edits (Git 2.23+)
# older Git:  git checkout -- environment-tenure_net.yml
git pull
```

**If you might need those Linux edits later:**

```bash
git stash push -m "rivanna env yml" -- environment-tenure_net.yml
git pull
```

You can compare later with `git stash show -p` or re-apply with `git stash pop` (only if you still want them).

**If you intentionally changed the file on Rivanna and want to keep them in history:** commit on Rivanna, then `git pull` (may require merge/rebase). That’s only worth it if those edits are real policy, not stray experiments.

For a single canonical `environment-tenure_net.yml`, **restore then pull** is usually what you want.

When I tried to do a git pull on Ivy_Net (the HPC) I got that screen shot as a response


---

_**Agent (claude-4.6-sonnet-medium-thinking)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The user built a Rivanna-friendly yml file on Mac, then committed and pushed the changes.</details></think>

_**Agent (claude-4.6-sonnet-medium-thinking)**_

What happened is separate from your Mac workflow.

**On Rivanna**, Git is comparing three things:

1. **What you last committed locally on Rivanna** (in `~/Ivy_Net`), and  
2. **What’s in your working tree right now** on Rivanna — and  
3. **What `git pull` would bring in** from `origin`.

The error means: **the file `environment-tenure_net.yml` on disk on Rivanna is not the same as Rivanna’s last committed version.** So there are **uncommitted local changes** on the HPC (from an old edit, a failed `sed`, copying a file by hand, or even editor/line-ending noise). Git will not overwrite that with the pull until you deal with those edits.

Your Mac commit/push is fine. Rivanna just has a **dirty** copy of that one file.

**What to do on Rivanna** (you almost certainly want the version from GitHub):

```bash
cd ~/Ivy_Net
git status
git diff environment-tenure_net.yml   # see what Rivanna thinks changed
git restore environment-tenure_net.yml
git pull
```

If `git restore` isn’t available, use: `git checkout -- environment-tenure_net.yml`

After that, `git pull` should succeed and you’ll have the same `environment-tenure_net.yml` as on your Mac.

**Optional:** `git diff` before `restore` is worth 10 seconds — if the only change is whitespace or line endings, you’ll see it and know you’re not losing something important.

_**Agent (claude-4.6-sonnet-medium-thinking)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The user provided another git status screenshot from Rivanna.</details></think>

_**Agent (claude-4.6-sonnet-medium-thinking)**_

`git status` matches what we thought: **Rivanna’s copy of `environment-tenure_net.yml` is modified in the working tree** (not staged), and your branch is **one commit behind `origin/main`** — that commit is almost certainly your Mac push.

**Do this on Rivanna:**

```bash
cd ~/Ivy_Net
git restore environment-tenure_net.yml
git pull
```

That throws away only the **local, uncommitted** edits to that file on the cluster, then fast-forwards to match GitHub.

Optional first step if you’re curious:

```bash
git diff environment-tenure_net.yml
```

If the diff is empty or only trivial, `restore` is safe. If you see real edits you care about, use `git stash push -- environment-tenure_net.yml` instead of `restore`, then `git pull`, then decide whether to `git stash pop` (usually you won’t need to for this workflow).

_**User**_

can't i just delete the yml file on Ivy_Net before the pull?

---

_**Agent (claude-4.6-sonnet-medium-thinking)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The user wants to know if the environment-tenure_net can be deleted. If the user deletes the file, Git will detect it as a deletion in the working directory compared to the committed version. This means `git status` will show "deleted: environment-tenure_net.yml," indicating a local modification. This deletion could potentially block a `git pull` in similar ways, as Git will see the file as removed from the working tree but still tracked in the repository. When you use `rm` on a tracked file, Git marks it for deletion in the next commit, which may cause conflicts during a pull if not handled properly. Deleting the file results in a dirty working tree, causing `git pull` to potentially fail with "Your local changes to the following files would be overwritten." This happens because Git detects the deletion as an unstaged change, similar to how it handles modified files. If a file is deleted using `rm`, Git marks it as deleted in the working tree, which can cause `git pull` to fail with errors about local changes or inability to merge. To undo the deletion, users can use `git restore file` or `git checkout HEAD -- file`. Alternatively, `git reset --hard` can be used as a more drastic measure. If the file is removed and then `git pull` is attempted, Git will likely detect the deletion as an unstaged change and may prevent the pull to avoid overwriting local modifications. For a deleted file, a `git pull` might succeed if the incoming commit modifies the file, but often it still blocks. A safer approach is to align Gits view: either use `git restore` after deleting, or simply run `rm` followed by `git checkout -- file` or `git restore file` to revert the deletion from the last committhis doesnt fetch the new commit yet.

Better workflow:
- `git restore environment-tenure_net.yml`  restores file to match HEAD (previous commit)
- `git pull`  fetches and merges the new file

If you only run `rm`:
- `git status` will show the file as deleted.</details></think>

_**Agent (claude-4.6-sonnet-medium-thinking)**_

You *can* delete it, but **`rm` alone usually does not fix the problem.** Git will then show the file as **deleted** in your working tree compared to your last commit — that is still a **local change**, and `git pull` may still refuse or behave oddly for the same reason.

**`git restore environment-tenure_net.yml`** is the right move: it puts the file back in sync with **your current Rivanna commit** and clears the “modified” state in one step, then **`git pull`** can bring in the version from GitHub.

So: deleting is a detour; **restore (or `git checkout -- environment-tenure_net.yml`) then pull** is the straightforward fix.

_**Agent (claude-4.6-sonnet-medium-thinking)**_

Yes. From Rivanna, with `myjob.slurm` in place (and your repo pulled in `~/Ivy_Net`):

```bash
cd ~/Ivy_Net   # or wherever the script lives
sbatch myjob.slurm
```

Check status with `squeue -u $USER` and the Slurm output file (e.g. `slurm-<jobid>.out` in the submit directory, unless you set `#SBATCH -o`).

**If `tenure_net` already exists from an old run**, `conda env create` may error with “already exists.” Then either remove it first (`conda env remove -n tenure_net`) or change the job line to:

`conda env update -f environment-tenure_net.yml --prune`

Otherwise you’re good to submit.

_**Agent (claude-4.6-sonnet-medium-thinking)**_

Here’s a concise path to get **SpecStory** talking to the **cloud**:

### 1. Account and extension
- Sign up / sign in at [cloud.specstory.com](https://cloud.specstory.com/).
- In **Cursor**, install the **SpecStory** extension from Cursor’s Extensions panel (not only the VS Code marketplace page), so it’s the Cursor build.

### 2. Local project
- Open your **project folder** (the one that has `.specstory/history/`). SpecStory writes history there; sync is tied to that project.

### 3. Configure and sync (in Cursor)
- **Command Palette** (`Cmd+Shift+P` on Mac):
  - **`SpecStory: Open Cloud Sync Configuration`** — review auth and project settings (good before first sync).
  - **`SpecStory: Sync with Cloud`** — runs auth if needed, then uploads sessions.  
- Sync is **not** continuous: **each upload is explicit** (no background sync unless they’ve changed that in a newer version—docs emphasize manual sync).

### 4. Optional: CLI
If you use the CLI in a terminal:

```bash
specstory login          # once per machine
specstory sync           # from the repo/project directory
```

### 5. Check it worked
- After sync, refresh **cloud.specstory.com** — you should see the project and sessions.

If something fails, typical issues are: wrong folder open (no `.specstory/history/`), not logged in, or firewall/VPN blocking the auth request. Say what you see (toast error, CLI output, or blank cloud) and we can narrow it down.

_**Agent (claude-4.6-sonnet-medium-thinking)**_

You’re already in the right place for **auth and project settings** — that **Cloud Sync** tab *is* the project settings UI. Your screenshot shows **Ivy_Net** as **Synced (Private)**, **Cloud Sync is Active**, last sync time, and **“All chats synced”**, so SpecStory is connected to **cloud.specstory.com** for that workspace.

### How to open it anytime
- **Command Palette** (`Cmd+Shift+P`) → **`SpecStory: Open Cloud Sync Configuration`**  
  That opens the same Cloud Sync / project dashboard you’re looking at.

Other useful commands (names may vary slightly by extension version):
- **`SpecStory: Sync with Cloud`** — manual push if you want to force a sync.
- Extension **settings**: `Cmd+,` → search **SpecStory** for toggles (e.g. auto-save to history).

### History folder vs cloud
- **Local:** `.specstory/history/` under whichever folder you have open — that’s normal; the extension writes there first.
- **Cloud:** What you see in that dashboard + [cloud.specstory.com](https://cloud.specstory.com/) after sync. Your UI says sync is on and chats are synced for **Ivy_Net**.

### If you use multiple roots (e.g. Dropbox `Cursor Workspace PDE` vs `Ivy_Net`)
Each workspace that has its own `.specstory/history/` is effectively a **separate SpecStory project**. Open the folder you care about, then **Open Cloud Sync Configuration** again — you may see different stats or need to enable sync per project.

So: auth/project settings = **Command Palette → `SpecStory: Open Cloud Sync Configuration`**; your screen already shows a healthy, synced setup for Ivy_Net.

It will be empty at first until you sync your histories and project.\n\nInfo\n\nSpecStory is local‑first. Nothing is synced until you explicitly turn on sync on your machine.\n\n## 2. The first Markdown file is created on save/auto-save.\n\n## 3. Authenticate your device for Cloud\n\nRun once per machine to allow uploads from the CLI/extension:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nspecstory login\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThis opens a browser window to complete a secure login. Your device stores a token locally and future syncs use it automatically.\n\nNote\n\nAuthentication enables sync, but does not upload anything by itself.\n\n## 4. Sync your first project\n\nYou control what (and when) to sync. Pick the path that matches your setup.\n\n### A) From Cursor / VS Code extensions\n\nThis will open a configuration screen where you can:\n\n- Ensure you're logged in\n- Enable sync for this project\n- Optionally enable sync for all future projects\n\n### B) From the CLI\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n# Sync all local sessions in the current repo/project\nspecstory sync\n\n# Sync a single session by ID\nspecstory sync claude -s \u003csession-uuid\u003e\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nAfter sync, refresh [cloud.specstory.com](https://cloud.specstory.com/), you should see the project and synced sessions.\n\n## 5. Run your first search\n\nApply filters:\n\n- Projects (multi‑select)\n- Time Range\n\nSearch combines keyword and semantic retrieval so you can quickly resurface reasoning, tradeoffs, or design threads.\n\nTip\n\nSearch runs across all projects you’ve synced, so it’s ideal for cross‑repo discovery.\n\n## 6. Current scope of collaboration\n\nSpecStory Cloud currently supports single‑user. Each developer can sync their own histories and search them centrally. Team workspaces, invitations, and role‑based access are on the roadmap.\n\nWhat you can do today:\n\n- Open a result to review the results.\n- Each teammate can sync their own&#96;.specstory/history/&#96; to Git for code‑adjacent review in PRs and docs.\n- Sessions can be exported or shared manually if needed.\n\n## Best practices\n\n- Start small with one repo: Sync one project first and validate capture → sync → search before rolling out to all repos.\n- Be intentional about what you sync: Version your&#96;.specstory/history/&#96; in Git and use Cloud for team-wide search.\n- Tag important sessions: Improves later search and retrieval.\n\n## Troubleshooting\n\nNo sessions appear after sync\n\n- Confirm&#96;.specstory/history/&#96; exists and contains Markdown files.\n- Run&#96;specstory login&#96; again if your token expired.\n- Re‑run&#96;specstory sync&#96; from the project root.\n\nCommand Palette action missing (Cursor/VS Code)\n\n- Verify the SpecStory extension is installed and enabled in that editor.\n- Restart the editor after installation.\n\nCLI can’t find Claude Code sessions\n\n- Ensure you launched Claude Code via&#96;specstory run&#96; so the wrapper can capture sessions.\n- Use&#96;specstory sync&#96; to convert existing session logs to Markdown.\n\nNeed help? _**Agent (claude-4.6-sonnet-medium-thinking)**_

Here’s a minimal path to get the **SpecStory CLI** working on your Mac (and a note for Linux/HPC).

### 1. Install (macOS — Homebrew)

```bash
brew tap specstoryai/tap
brew update
brew install specstory
```

Confirm:

```bash
specstory version
specstory check
```

If `specstory` is “command not found”, your Homebrew `bin` may not be on `PATH` — fix your shell PATH or run `brew doctor`.

### 2. Log in (once per machine)

```bash
specstory login
```

That should open a browser to finish auth and store a token locally. Same account idea as the Cursor extension.

### 3. Sync from a project that has history

```bash
cd /path/to/your/project   # folder that contains .specstory/history/
specstory sync
```

Use the **repo root** where `.specstory/history/` actually lives (e.g. your Ivy_Net or dissertation workspace folder), not a random parent.

### 4. If something fails

- **`specstory check`** — sanity checks from the installer.
- **No sessions** — ensure `.specstory/history/` has `.md` files; do a short chat in Cursor so something is saved if the folder is empty.
- **Auth errors** — run `specstory login` again.
- **Help** — `specstory --help` and `specstory sync --help` (flags can change by version).

### Rivanna / Linux

Homebrew is mostly Mac. On Linux they point you to [GitHub releases](https://github.com/specstoryai/getspecstory/releases) and their [installation guide](https://docs.specstory.com/specstory/introduction). Install the binary there, then same `specstory login` / `specstory sync` from the project directory.

You don’t *need* the CLI if the Cursor extension already syncs — the CLI is for terminals, automation, and syncing from a shell without opening the UI.

_**Agent (claude-4.6-sonnet-medium-thinking)**_

`zsh: command not found: brew` means **Homebrew isn’t available in this shell** — either it isn’t installed, or it isn’t on your `PATH` (less common on a normal Mac login).

You can do either of the following.

### Option 1: Install Homebrew, then SpecStory (what the docs assume)

1. Install Homebrew (official one-liner): [https://brew.sh](https://brew.sh) — run the script it shows, then **open a new terminal** (or `source` your profile).

2. Confirm:

```bash
which brew
brew --version
```

3. Then:

```bash
brew tap specstoryai/tap
brew install specstory
specstory version
```

If you’re on a **managed machine** where you can’t install Homebrew, use Option 2.

### Option 2: CLI without Homebrew (GitHub release)

1. Open **[releases](https://github.com/specstoryai/getspecstory/releases)** for `specstoryai/getspecstory`.

2. Under **Assets**, download the **macOS** archive for your CPU (Apple Silicon → usually `arm64` / “aarch64”; Intel → `amd64` / “x86_64”).

3. Unzip/extract, move the `specstory` binary somewhere on your `PATH` (e.g. `~/bin` or `/usr/local/bin`), and `chmod +x` if needed.

4. Test:

```bash
specstory version
specstory login
cd /path/to/project   # must contain .specstory/history/
specstory sync
```

### If you only need cloud sync from Cursor

You don’t need the CLI at all: the extension + **`SpecStory: Sync with Cloud`** is enough. The CLI is mainly for **terminal agents** and **scripted** `specstory sync`.

**Note:** Your prompt shows `Cursor Workspace PDE` — that’s fine. The missing piece is only **Homebrew** (or a manual binary); `tenure_net` conda environment doesn’t affect `brew`.

_**Agent (claude-4.6-sonnet-medium-thinking)**_

Nothing failed in the install — Homebrew finished with **“Installation successful!”** The problem is that **your shell doesn’t know where `brew` lives yet**, because the **“Next steps”** block wasn’t applied (or this terminal was opened before that).

On Apple Silicon, `brew` is **`/opt/homebrew/bin/brew`**. Until `PATH` includes that directory, `zsh` reports `command not found: brew`.

**Do this once (matches what the installer printed):**

```bash
echo >> /Users/charleslevine/.zprofile
echo 'eval "$(/opt/homebrew/bin/brew shellenv)"' >> /Users/charleslevine/.zprofile
eval "$(/opt/homebrew/bin/brew shellenv)"
```

Then **open a new terminal tab/window**, or run `source ~/.zprofile`, and check:

```bash
which brew
brew --version
```

**Optional:** `brew which` isn’t the usual check — use `which brew` (shell) or `brew --version` (Homebrew).

After `brew` works, continue with `brew tap specstoryai/tap` and `brew install specstory`.

_**Agent (claude-4.6-sonnet-medium-thinking)**_

Here’s the CLI path that matches how SpecStory is designed to work.

### 1. One-time auth (per machine)
```bash
specstory login
```
If you’re already logged in from the extension, this may just refresh or succeed quickly.

### 2. Sync from the **project root**
The CLI expects to be run from the folder that contains **`.specstory/history/`** (same place the extension uses).

```bash
cd "/path/to/Cursor Workspace PDE"   # or Ivy_Net — wherever that .specstory folder lives
specstory sync
```

That walks the local history (including anything under `.specstory/history/`) and uploads sessions to the cloud. **CODA**, **SCOUT**, and **PEER** are almost certainly just different sessions or subfolders under `history/`; **one `specstory sync` from that repo root should cover all of them** unless SpecStory split them across different workspaces.

### 3. If those names are **three different repos** each with its own `.specstory/`
Repeat for each repo:

```bash
cd /path/to/repo-a && specstory sync
cd /path/to/repo-b && specstory sync
cd /path/to/repo-c && specstory sync
```

### 4. Optional: one session only
If you ever need a single session by id:

```bash
specstory sync -s <session-uuid>
```
(Use `specstory sync --help` on your install — flag names can vary slightly by version.)

### 5. Confirm
Refresh [cloud.specstory.com](https://cloud.specstory.com/) after `specstory sync` completes.

**Note:** Cloud sync is **explicit** — run `specstory sync` whenever you want uploads; it doesn’t run continuously in the background unless you’ve automated it with cron or similar.

_**Agent (claude-4.6-sonnet-medium-thinking)**_

Here’s what’s going on, in plain terms.

### Why `specstory sync` complained

**Bare `specstory sync` is for *terminal* coding agents**, not the Cursor **chat panel**.

It looks for things like **Claude Code**, **Codex CLI**, **Cursor CLI** (`cursor-agent` in the terminal), **Droid**, **Gemini CLI**, etc. If you haven’t run those from a project (or there’s no session data for them), you get exactly: *“No coding agent activity found for this project directory.”*

That message does **not** mean your `.specstory/history/` folder is wrong. It means: **the CLI isn’t the path for “CODA / SCOUT / PEER” in the editor.**

### What CODA, SCOUT, and PEER actually are

Those names are **Cursor UI chats / agent tabs** in the editor. SpecStory’s **extension** is what ties into that: it reads Cursor’s local chat store (`state.vscdb` on macOS) and writes **Markdown under `.specstory/history/`** (your `…-coda.md`, `…-scout.md`, `…-peer.md` files). You may not remember “creating” them — **the extension generates them** when it saves/export those sessions.

### How to sync *those* conversations to the cloud

Use the **editor**, not generic CLI sync:

1. Open the **same project folder** in Cursor (the one that contains `.specstory`).
2. **Command Palette** (`Cmd+Shift+P`) → **`SpecStory: Sync with Cloud`**.

That’s the supported path for **Cursor IDE** history. Your Cloud Sync screen that already said “All chats synced” is this pipeline.

**Do not rely on bare `specstory sync`** for Composer/CODA/SCOUT/PEER unless SpecStory changes the product; use **extension sync** (or whatever “Sync with Cloud” maps to in your version).

### If you want the **CLI** story to be relevant

Then you’re in a **different** workflow: **Cursor in the terminal**, wrapped by SpecStory, e.g.:

```bash
specstory run cursor    # Cursor CLI (cursor-agent), not the IDE chat tabs
specstory sync cursor   # syncs those *CLI* sessions from ~/.cursor/chats/ (see docs)
```

That is **not** the same as CODA/SCOUT/PEER in the sidebar. Docs explicitly pair editor history with CLI transcripts as separate sources.

### “Starting fresh” — collecting CODA / SCOUT / PEER

1. Keep the **SpecStory extension** enabled for this workspace.
2. Leave **auto-save / cloud** settings as you prefer in **SpecStory: Open Cloud Sync Configuration**.
3. Use your **normal Cursor chats** (CODA, SCOUT, PEER). New turns should keep producing/updating files under **`.specstory/history/`**.
4. When you want cloud copies: **`SpecStory: Sync with Cloud`** (not bare `specstory sync` for IDE chats).

So: **extension = IDE agents (CODA/SCOUT/PEER) + `.specstory/history/` + cloud; CLI `specstory sync` = terminal agents unless you’re using the Cursor **CLI** subcommands.** That’s why your folder looked fine while the CLI still warned you.

---

_**Agent (claude-4.6-sonnet-medium-thinking)**_

SpecStory’s commands were renamed/reorganized in newer builds, so **“SpecStory: Sync with Cloud” often doesn’t exist anymore.** Your palette list is the current shape.

Use these instead:

### 1. **`SpecStory: Open Cloud Sync Configuration`**
Opens the Cloud Sync dashboard (the one where you saw “Cloud Sync is Active,” last sync time, etc.). That screen is where **login, project link, and sync** usually live now—look for a **Sync** / **Sync now** action or rely on **toggle** (below).

### 2. **`SpecStory: Toggle Cloud Sync for this project`**
Turns cloud sync **on** for this workspace. With sync on, the extension can upload when you save or on a schedule depending on version—pair this with the configuration view.

### 3. **`SpecStory: Save AI Chat History`**
Forces a fresh write to **`.specstory/history/`** before you expect anything to upload. Use this if you want to be sure local Markdown is current.

### 4. **`SpecStory: Launch CLI Sync (Claude Code, Codex CLI, etc...)`**
This is the **terminal-agent** path (`specstory sync` style), **not** the main path for normal Cursor IDE chats. Use it only if you’re syncing CLI sessions.

**Practical sequence:**  
**Save AI Chat History** → **Open Cloud Sync Configuration** → confirm you’re logged in and **toggle Cloud Sync on** for this project → use any **Sync** control on that page if shown.

If nothing uploads, check **Open Cloud Sync Configuration** for errors and that you’re in the **same folder** that contains `.specstory/history/`. The missing “Sync with Cloud” label is a docs/naming drift issue, not something you did wrong.

_**Agent (claude-4.6-sonnet-medium-thinking)**_

**`specstory run cursor`** starts the **Cursor CLI** (the terminal agent, typically `cursor-agent`) **through SpecStory’s wrapper** so your session is captured and written under **`.specstory/history/`** as Markdown (and can be synced to SpecStory Cloud like other CLI agents).

So it’s for **AI coding in the terminal**, not for the **Cursor editor** chat / Composer / CODA–SCOUT–PEER tabs.

**Rough mental model**

| Command | What it runs |
|--------|----------------|
| Normal Cursor app | IDE chats, agents in the UI → SpecStory **extension** reads `state.vscdb` → `.specstory/history/` |
| `specstory run cursor` | **Cursor CLI** in the shell → SpecStory tracks that CLI session → `.specstory/history/` |

You need the **Cursor CLI** installed and on your `PATH` (per SpecStory docs: `cursor-agent --help`). If you only use Cursor in the GUI, you usually **don’t** need `specstory run cursor`; the extension path is the one that matches that workflow.

_**User**_

So can you explain that more thoroughly.  I thought that specstory was writing a history of my conversations so that I could access them across different workspaces


---

_**Agent (claude-4.6-sonnet-medium-thinking)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The user wants a thorough explanation of how SpecStory works - specifically around the history files and cross-workspace access.</details></think>

_**Agent (claude-4.6-sonnet-medium-thinking)**_

Yes, that's the goal — but there are **two separate pipelines** in SpecStory, and they don't fully overlap. Here's the full picture:

---

## How SpecStory actually works

### Pipeline 1: The Cursor Extension (your current setup)

The **SpecStory extension** in Cursor does this:
1. Reads Cursor's internal chat database (`state.vscdb`) on your Mac
2. Writes those conversations as `.md` files into **`.specstory/history/`** inside whichever project you have open
3. If cloud sync is on → uploads them to **cloud.specstory.com**

**Key limitation:** The history files live **inside your project folder**. So:
- CODA, SCOUT, PEER chats → saved in `Cursor Workspace PDE/.specstory/history/`
- If you open a **different** project/folder, the extension writes to **that** folder's `.specstory/history/`
- They don't automatically cross-reference or merge across projects

### Pipeline 2: The CLI (`specstory run`, `specstory sync`, `specstory check`)

This is **entirely separate** — designed for **terminal-based** AI agents (Claude Code, Codex CLI, etc. running in a shell). It has **no awareness** of your Cursor IDE chat tabs (CODA/SCOUT/PEER).

`specstory check` reports "no activity" because it's only looking for **terminal agent** session data, not the `.md` files the extension already wrote.

---

## How cross-workspace / cross-device access actually works

The **cloud** is the bridge:

```
Mac Cursor IDE  →  extension writes .md  →  cloud sync  →  cloud.specstory.com
                                                              ↑
Rivanna / other device  →  specstory login  →  can read/search there
```

So the **extension + cloud sync** is the correct path for your CODA/SCOUT/PEER history. The `.md` files in `.specstory/history/` are the **local copy**; [cloud.specstory.com](https://cloud.specstory.com) is the **cross-device** access point.

---

## The gap you're probably hitting

Your `.md` files exist locally. Whether they're **on the cloud** depends on:
- **Cloud sync being on** for this project (you saw "Synced (Private)" — looks good)
- Whether the extension has actually **uploaded** those specific files yet

**Check:** Go to [cloud.specstory.com](https://cloud.specstory.com) → do you see an **Ivy_Net** or **Cursor Workspace PDE** project with those 5 sessions listed? If yes, you're set and can search them from anywhere. If not, run **Command Palette → `SpecStory: Save AI Chat History`** then check the Cloud Sync configuration page for errors.

---

## Bottom line

| What you want | How to get it |
|---|---|
| Local history of IDE chats | Extension → `.specstory/history/` ✅ already working |
| Cloud backup / search | Extension cloud sync → cloud.specstory.com (need to confirm uploads completed) |
| Cross-workspace search | cloud.specstory.com after both projects have synced |
| Terminal agent capture | CLI `specstory run` (separate, not needed for IDE chats) |

The **CLI tools (`specstory check`, `specstory sync`)** are essentially irrelevant for your current workflow — you're using the IDE, not terminal agents. The extension + cloud is your path.

---

_**User**_

OK, I see them - Yay!  But how often does it synch?  How can I ensure it synchs?


---

_**Agent (claude-4.6-sonnet-medium-thinking)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The user can see their sessions on cloud.specstory.com. They want to know how often it syncs and how to ensure it syncs.</details></think>

_**Agent (claude-4.6-sonnet-medium-thinking)**_

The sync frequency isn't publicly documented with a specific interval — SpecStory's docs consistently say **"no background uploads; every sync requires explicit action."** In practice the extension *may* auto-sync when you save history, but you can't count on it.

### How to ensure it syncs

**Most reliable: manual trigger after important sessions**

Command Palette (`Cmd+Shift+P`):
1. **`SpecStory: Save AI Chat History`** — writes fresh `.md` files locally from the current chat state
2. Then check **`SpecStory: Open Cloud Sync Configuration`** — the "Last Sync" timestamp updates when an upload completes

Do this at the end of any session you care about (end of a PEER chat, end of a work session, etc.).

---

### Make it a habit (or automate it)

**Option 1 — Manual ritual (simplest)**
Run `SpecStory: Save AI Chat History` before closing Cursor. 30 seconds, zero overhead.

**Option 2 — Automate with a shell alias + cron**
Since the CLI *can* do extension-style sync from the project folder in newer versions:

```bash
cd "/path/to/Cursor Workspace PDE" && specstory sync
```

Put that in a cron job or a shell alias you run periodically.

**Option 3 — Git as a backup**
Since the `.md` files are already in your repo and tracked by git, every `git push` gives you a **second backup** on GitHub alongside the cloud. This is actually useful — the `.md` files are readable without SpecStory at all.

---

### Quick rule of thumb

> After any important conversation: **`Save AI Chat History`**, then **`git add . && git commit && git push`** if you want belt-and-suspenders coverage.

That covers both cloud.specstory.com **and** GitHub.

_**Agent (claude-4.6-sonnet-medium-thinking)**_

Almost — but there are a few differences worth knowing:

### `git add .` vs `git add -A`

| | What it stages |
|---|---|
| `git add .` | All changes **in the current directory and below** (new, modified, deleted) |
| `git add -A` | All changes **in the entire repo** (regardless of where you run it from) |

For your workflow (running from repo root) they behave **identically**. `-A` is the safer habit if you ever `cd` into a subfolder before committing.

### `git commit` vs `git commit -m "message"`

- `git commit` with **no `-m`** opens your default text editor (usually vim or nano) for you to type the message. If you haven't configured an editor it can feel stuck.
- `git commit -m "your message"` does it inline, no editor. **This is what you want for quick commits.**

So the one-liner with a message would be:

```bash
git add -A && git commit -m "save specstory history" && git push
```

### `git push` vs `git push origin main`

- `git push` pushes the **current branch to its configured upstream** (works fine once upstream is set)
- `git push origin main` is explicit — same result if you're on `main` tracking `origin/main`

Note: it's `git push origin main` not `git push main origin` (remote comes first, then branch).

### Your reliable one-liner

```bash
git add -A && git commit -m "save specstory history" && git push
```

That's equivalent to your three separate commands and is safe to run from the repo root.
